title: My $14.99 Costco Lavender
subtitle: Planting meaning and watching it grow

blocks.content: When I went back home this past reading week, I distinctly remember one moment. I had gone for a walk in the morning, and as I was rounding the corner to our front door, one of the plants in our front yard caught my eye. It was a tiny bush of lavender nestled between some of our larger plants. I stopped, admiring the green stems gently swaying in the breeze. Winter was almost over, so there were speckles of purple blossoms that were just beginning to show. I must have stood there for at least 10 minutes.

To give some context, I had planted this lavender nearly two years ago. It was the only contribution I had made to this garden. I recall quite clearly the day I planted it. I went to Costco in search of a plant, and when I stumbled upon this lavender sitting in a pot with a sign beside it that read $14.99, I knew it was the one. My motivation for planting flowers had been planned weeks in advance. I had chosen to do my capstone project on focus, and as the final experiment, I did a digital detox for seven days, documenting everything that happened. To make it more interesting, I thought of seven challenges I would do over the course of the week that trained my focus abilities, including counting rice, reading TikTok’s terms and conditions, and cutting paper squares. My final activity was to plant a flower.

As I took my lavender home and planted it, I was overcome with a sense of happiness. I wrote extensively about the lavender in my journal that day, finding the idea of intentionally creating symbolism quite fascinating:

*“It is proof that past portions of our lives can live on and acts as a reminder of the memories created in the past week. Previous chapters of our lives, although they end, don’t just stop there. Like the lavender, they continue growing, impacting the subsequent chapters to come. While it might have been planted at a certain time, the planting part is only the beginning.”*

Underlying my grand definitions was also a burning curiosity. I wondered if it was possible to create meaning in my life so deliberately. At the end of the day, wasn’t meaning derived? Wasn’t it retrospective? Could I even create meaning like this?

In many ways, it did feel quite artificial. I had planned this symbolism out to serve an intended purpose. I chose the plant and the exact place to put it. Forced this lavender to become a part of this garden. Like the intended meaning behind it, would it even spread its roots in this predetermined soil and grow?

This explanation, in fact, hits on a core difference between optimism and pessimism that I’ve come to see people believe. Optimism has to be inserted into a situation, made to become true. Pessimism is rooted more or less in what something is. Not how something should be or could be. But what it will be.

I’ve often thought about this difference between the two. How people equate optimism with naivety because of this very premise. Especially over the course of the next year after planting this lavender, I began to believe it.

Looking back, the timing of planting this lavender was quite ironic. It was the end of my grade 11 summer break, and grade 12 was right around the corner. I did not know it then, but grade 12 would be the most challenging year physically and mentally for me. As if to directly destroy my symbolic analysis, the lavender began to die within a month of planting it. Despite my attempts to water it consistently, parts of it just kept on dying. By winter, it had basically all shrivelled up. Coincidentally, that was also when the worst of grade 12 happened. It seemed that whatever higher power—whether it be the universe, nature, or God—was showing me the consequences of being optimistic. How meaning and symbolism could not just be inserted, like this lavender was into our garden. Meaning and optimism were not in my control.

However, what I’ve come to realize is that life likes to be unpredictable. It likes to toy with your emotions, squander your hopes and dreams, kill your lavender plant, and then suddenly flip the narrative entirely.

After a couple of months, I had forgotten about the lavender. As with seemingly futile attempts to create meaning in one’s life, it became something that slowly faded away from my consciousness, crowded out by the new worries and stresses of each day. By then, I was nearing the end of grade 12. All the negativity had compounded, leading me to write possibly my most pessimistic journal entry. Although I don’t intend to show it, the main theme was how I felt I was always second place in all areas of my life. A pessimist would have loved that journal entry. But what was ironic about that specific entry was the motivation I had to write it. I had been talking to a friend, and they mentioned watching the video I had made about the digital detox week for my capstone. They asked specifically about the lavender, to which I confidently responded, “It’s dead.” Just for fun though, we decided to go check on it anyway. To my surprise, the lavender was very much alive. At least half of it seemed to be. Upon closer inspection, I realized the dead parts were slowly decomposing, making way for a new small batch of fresh green stems that were pushing against the withered parts, jostling for sunlight.
I’ll include an excerpt from that journal entry:

*“The colour of it has completely come back, and as I write, it has surpassed the height it initially was when I first purchased it. Past me was correct in the significance of the lavender. Its symbolic meaning of how our past chapters continue to influence the life we live now. But as I have journeyed this past 10 months, I’ve realized it means much more than just that. It also shows resilience. It shows the beauty in imperfection. The plant didn’t just continually grow. It shrivelled up. But more importantly, it was able to grow again, to bloom despite the harsh conditions and neglect it faced. So I thank past me for planting that lavender. I thank him for the light it has provided me in a time where it has been quite dark. I just hope I fare the same as the lavender.”*

During those 10 minutes I stood looking at the lavender recently, I thought about everything I just wrote. It had been seven months since that journal entry. A lot had changed. Much, for the better. It might seem silly to base your entire view and justification for always being optimistic on some lavender. But as I turned around and opened my door, I was so happy it was my justification. Because no matter how long winter is, spring will always come.
